root_text.an5.47:2.2: Idha, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako saddho hoti, saddahati tathāgatassa bodhiṁ:
root_text.an5.47:2.3: ‘itipi so bhagavā …pe… satthā devamanussānaṁ buddho bhagavā’ti.
root_text.an5.47:3.2: Idha, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako pāṇātipātā paṭivirato hoti …pe… surāmerayamajjapamādaṭṭhānā paṭivirato hoti.
root_text.an5.47:4.2: Idha, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako bahussuto hoti …pe… diṭṭhiyā suppaṭividdho.
root_text.an5.47:5.2: Idha, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako vigatamalamaccherena cetasā agāraṁ ajjhāvasati muttacāgo payatapāṇi vosaggarato yācayogo dānasaṁvibhāgarato.
root_text.an5.47:6.2: Idha, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako paññavā hoti, udayatthagāminiyā paññāya samannāgato ariyāya nibbedhikāya sammā dukkhakkhayagāminiyā.

translation_text.an5.47:1.1: “Mendicants, there are these five kinds of wealth.
translation_text.an5.47:1.3: The wealth of faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom.
translation_text.an5.47:2.1: And what is the wealth of faith?
translation_text.an5.47:2.2: It’s when a noble disciple has faith in the Realized One’s awakening:
translation_text.an5.47:2.3: ‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’
translation_text.an5.47:2.4: This is called the wealth of faith.
translation_text.an5.47:3.1: And what is the wealth of ethics?
translation_text.an5.47:3.2: It’s when a noble disciple doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume beer, wine, and liquor intoxicants.
translation_text.an5.47:3.3: This is called the wealth of ethics.
translation_text.an5.47:4.1: And what is the wealth of learning?
translation_text.an5.47:4.2: It’s when a noble disciple is very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s totally full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, reciting them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically.
translation_text.an5.47:4.3: This is called the wealth of learning.
translation_text.an5.47:5.1: And what is the wealth of generosity?
translation_text.an5.47:5.2: It’s when a noble disciple lives at home rid of the stain of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share.
translation_text.an5.47:5.3: This is called the wealth of generosity.
translation_text.an5.47:6.1: And what is the wealth of wisdom?
translation_text.an5.47:6.2: It’s when a noble disciple is wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering.
translation_text.an5.47:6.3: This is called the wealth of wisdom.
translation_text.an5.47:6.4: These are the five kinds of wealth.
translation_text.an5.47:7.1: Whoever has faith in the Realized One,
translation_text.an5.47:7.4: praised and loved by the noble ones;
translation_text.an5.47:8.1: who has confidence in the Saṅgha,
translation_text.an5.47:8.4: their life is not in vain.
translation_text.an5.47:9.1: So let the wise devote themselves
translation_text.an5.47:9.3: confidence, and insight into the teaching,
translation_text.an5.47:9.4: remembering the instructions of the Buddhas.”
